summary: Gruber examines Charlie Warzel's NYT piece connecting Facebook's platform design to the FBI-foiled plot to kidnap Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer. He plays devil's advocate, suggesting that social media may have revealed pre-existing extremism rather than creating it. He highlights a Michigan sheriff's disturbing defense of the plotters as evidence of deep-rooted militia culture. While acknowledging Facebook's culpability for fostering extremist engagement, Gruber argues there's a 'shoot-the-messenger' aspect to blaming platforms entirely. He suggests the real problem is generations of denial about pervasive far-right extremism in American culture.
key_points: Facebook's platform, when working as designed, naturally accelerates the erosion of shared reality and democratic norms
Social media may have revealed pre-existing American extremism rather than creating it — lifting a lid on deep social rot
The political divide is re-sorting from left/right to sane/crazy
A Michigan sheriff's defense of the kidnapping plotters exemplifies how normalized militia culture has become
Blaming Facebook entirely has a shoot-the-messenger quality; the underlying extremism predates social media
money_quotes: Perhaps Facebook et al. have less exacerbated kookery — particularly but not exclusively on the right wing — than revealed what has always been there, under the surface of American culture and politics.
We're re-sorting from a primarily left/right political divide to one that's primarily sane/crazy.

Trust me, I think Facebook's leadership is deeply culpable for looking the other way, ignoring the obvious consequences of building a system that fosters 'engagement' by allowing like-minded extremists to find each other.
There's a shoot-the-messenger aspect to saying this is all Facebook's fault.
We've been in denial for generations about how pervasive and dangerous this 'militia' culture is.
Facebook, when it's working as designed, is a natural accelerating force in the erosion of our shared reality and, with it, our democratic norms.
key_insight: Facebook deserves blame for amplifying extremism, but scapegoating the platform risks perpetuating our denial about how deep and pervasive far-right militia culture has always been in America.
